meditation.noRecords: No meditation records yet. Start meditating!
meditation.breathingTip1: 👉 Breathe in slowly through your nose, hold gently, then exhale through your mouth
meditation.breathingTip2: 💡 This calms your nervous system and reduces cravings naturally
meditation.listenToMusic: Listen to ambient music while meditating

meditations.title: Breath Awareness
meditations.description: Start with basic breath awareness to calm your mind.
meditations.guide: Take a comfortable seat. Close your eyes. Breathe naturally through your nose. Count each exhale from 1 to 10, then start again. If your mind wanders, that's okay - just return to counting. Continue for 5 minutes. This simple practice helps calm anxiety and cravings.

meditations.title: Body Scan
meditations.description: Release tension from your body systematically.
meditations.guide: Lie down or sit comfortably. Close your eyes. Start at the top of your head. Notice any tension. As you breathe, imagine releasing that tension with each exhale. Slowly move down: forehead, jaw, neck, shoulders, arms, chest, stomach, legs, feet. Spend 10-15 seconds on each area. This technique reduces physical stress from withdrawal.

meditations.title: Craving Release
meditations.guide: Sit comfortably. Close your eyes. Notice any craving in your body without judgment. Don't try to fight it. Observe: where is it? What does it feel like? Does it have a color, shape, or temperature? As you breathe, imagine the craving as a wave passing through you. Watch it rise, peak, and fall. Remember: cravings are temporary. This too shall pass. They fade naturally within 15-20 minutes.

meditations.title: Loving Kindness
meditations.description: Cultivate compassion and self-love during recovery.
meditations.guide: Sit comfortably. Close your eyes. Place hand on heart. Say silently: "May I be peaceful. May I be healthy. May I be happy. May I be free from addiction." Repeat slowly, feeling each word. Then extend: "May others also find peace and healing." This practice builds self-compassion and motivation for recovery.

tips: Start with just 5 minutes daily. Consistency matters more than duration.
Practice at the same time each day to build a habit.
Cravings peak 15-20 minutes. Meditate through the peak.
Use meditation when you feel most tempted.
Your mind will wander - that's normal. Gently bring it back.
Track your progress. Notice how cravings become easier to handle.

postureTipsList.label: Spine
postureTipsList.text: Keep it straight but not rigid - imagine a string from your tail to the top of your head

postureTipsList.label: Shoulders
postureTipsList.text: Relax down and back, away from your ears

postureTipsList.label: Hands
postureTipsList.text: Rest on your knees or lap, palms up or down as comfortable

postureTipsList.label: Chin
postureTipsList.text: Tuck slightly, eyes looking down naturally (open or closed both fine)

postureTipsList.label: Feet
postureTipsList.text: If sitting, plant firmly on ground. If lying down, let them naturally fall outward

crisis.needHelp: すぐに助けが必要ですか?
crisis.breathe: 呼吸する時間をください
crisis.grounding: グラウンディングテクニック

selfAssessment.quizSubtitle: Understand where you are on your recovery journey
selfAssessment.resultsSubtitle: Understanding where you are is the first step toward recovery
introItems: ✓ Assesses your relationship with your addiction
importantItems: 🔒 Your responses are private and never saved to a server unless you choose
📝 This is NOT a medical diagnosis—only professionals can diagnose addiction
💚 This quiz is designed to be supportive, not judgmental
🎯 Results provide personalized guidance for your recovery
selfAssessment.introMessage: Taking this quiz is an act of courage and self-awareness. Whatever your results, know that recovery is possible. Let's find out where you are so you can move forward.
selfAssessment.closingMessage: Your journey to recovery starts with honesty—which you've just demonstrated. Every step forward, no matter how small, matters. You've got this. 💪
rememberItems: This is not a diagnosis. Only qualified professionals can diagnose addiction.
Recovery is always possible. People have recovered from far worse than your current situation.
You deserve support. Seeking help is a sign of strength, not weakness.
Today is the perfect day to start. Not tomorrow, not next week—now.
selfAssessment.functioningDescription: You may be experiencing "functional addiction"—where you maintain external responsibilities while addiction grows invisibly. This is critical information. The sooner you act, the better your recovery will be.
selfAssessment.recommendations: 📚 Recommended Reading Based on Your Results:

descriptions.atRisk: Your responses suggest you may be in the early stages of developing a dependency. While your current use may seem manageable, it's important to be mindful of patterns that could develop over time.
descriptions.earlyStage: Your responses suggest you may have early-stage dependency. The good news is that at this stage, intervention is often most effective. Many people recover easily when they seek help early.
descriptions.moderate: Your responses indicate moderate addiction with meaningful impacts on your life. This is a critical moment where recovery is absolutely possible, but it will require real commitment and support.
descriptions.severe: Your responses suggest severe addiction with substantial impacts on your health, relationships, and life. This is serious, but recovery is still possible. Many people have come back from this point.
descriptions.critical: Your responses indicate critical-level addiction. Your life is in immediate danger. But please know: recovery is possible, and Back on Track is here to support you every step of the way.

selfAssessment.functioning: 🚨 **Important**: Your responses suggest you may be a "functioning user"—appearing to maintain life responsibilities while addiction grows. This is particularly dangerous because the hidden damage accelerates while you seem fine. This is exactly why early intervention matters.

frequency.question: How often do you use your substance/engage in the behavior?

impact_relationships.question: Has your use affected your relationships with family or friends?

impact_work.question: How has your use affected your job, school, or responsibilities?

quit_attempts.question: Have you tried to stop or cut down?

options.text: Tried a few times, with some success
Tried many times, failed each time

withdrawal.question: Do you experience withdrawal symptoms when you stop or reduce use?

options.text: Severe withdrawal that makes quitting very difficult

tolerance.question: Has the amount you need increased over time?

options.text: Need much more to feel any effect

health_safety.question: Has your use caused health or safety problems?

options.text: Serious health crisis or risk of overdose

time_spent.question: How much time do you spend on your addiction or recovering from it?

options.text: An hour or less per day
6+ hours per day or consumes most of my time

consequences.question: Do you continue despite knowing the negative consequences?

options.text: I stop when I realize the harm
I often continue despite knowing the harm
Nothing stops me, even facing disaster

functioning.question: 使用に関係なく、基本的な生活責任をまだ管理していますか?

options.text: すべてが順調に進んでいます
ほとんどの場合、更新しています
努力しながら追っています
物事をかろうじて一緒に保つ
生活が崩壊しています

header.title: Make Your Plan & Get Ready to Stop
header.subtitle: Preparation is the foundation of successful recovery. Use this guide to set yourself up for success.

motivation.title: Why Preparation Matters
motivation.mainMessage: Recovery isn't something that just happens—it's something you build.
motivation.description: The time you spend preparing now will directly determine your success. People who plan their recovery are significantly more likely to stick with it through the difficult first days.
benefits: Preparation reduces impulsive decisions when you're struggling
It builds confidence and commitment before you start
It identifies potential obstacles before they catch you off-guard
It ensures you have support systems in place from day one
It demonstrates to yourself that you're serious about change
motivation.insight: 💡 Every minute you spend planning now saves you hours of struggle later.

assess.title: 1. Assess Your Current Situation
assess.intro: Before you stop, understand where you are:

questions.frequency: How often am I using/engaging? (daily, weekly, multiple times daily)
questions.money: How much money am I spending per day/week?
questions.time: What time of day do I use most?
questions.triggers: What situations trigger my use? (stress, boredom, social, emotional)
questions.impact: How has this addiction affected my health, relationships, work, finances?
questions.obstacles: What have been my obstacles to quitting in the past?

assess.tip: Tip: Use this app to track your current usage for a few days before you plan to quit. Real data is more powerful than guesses.

quitDate.title: 2. Set a Specific Quit Date
quitDate.mainAdvice: Pick a date within the next 1-2 weeks. Give yourself enough time to prepare, but not so much that you lose motivation.
goodTiming: ✓ Good timing: Pick a day after a stressful event passes (not during)
✓ Good timing: Pick a day when you have support available
badTiming: ✗ Bad timing: Don't pick when you're already stressed or emotional
✗ Bad timing: Don't pick when you'll be alone with easy access
quitDate.finalAdvice: Once you've picked your date, mark it in your calendar. Tell people about it. Post it somewhere you'll see it daily. Make it real.

triggers.title: 3. Identify Your Triggers
triggers.intro: Triggers are situations, emotions, or people that make you want to use. Knowing them in advance means you can plan for them.

emotional.title: Emotional Triggers
emotional.description: Stress, boredom, loneliness, anger, anxiety, sadness... what emotions drive your use?

social.title: Social Triggers
social.description: Specific people, places, or groups. Do you use with certain friends? In certain locations?

environmental.title: Environmental Triggers
environmental.description: Times of day, locations, objects. Does being in your room trigger you? Late nights?

habitual.title: Habitual Triggers
habitual.description: Things you've always done together. Morning coffee ritual? After work routine?

triggers.action: Action: For each trigger, write down a specific plan for what you'll do instead.

support.title: 4. Build Your Support Network
support.intro: You cannot do this alone. Successful recovery requires connection and support. Build your network now, before you quit.

people.title: 👥 People to Tell
items: At least one person you trust completely who you can call anytime
Someone who won't judge you if you struggle
Someone who will celebrate your wins
Consider telling a therapist, counselor, or doctor

resources.title: 📱 Support Resources
items: Support groups (AA, NA, SMART Recovery, etc.)
Therapy or counseling services
This app - use ALL its features (Diary, Mood, Meditation)
Helplines and crisis text lines

professional.title: 🏥 Professional Help
items: Medical withdrawal support if needed for alcohol or drugs
Medication-assisted treatment if recommended
Mental health treatment for underlying issues
Don't try to do this without help if it's dangerous

support.action: Write down 3-5 specific people you can call when it gets hard. Get their numbers now and save them in your phone.

coping.title: 5. Plan Your Coping Strategies
coping.intro: Cravings will come. You need concrete tools ready BEFORE they arrive.

meditation.title: 🧘 Meditation & Mindfulness
meditation.description: Use this app's Meditation feature. Even 2 minutes helps.

distraction.title: 🎮 Distraction Strategies
distraction.description: Use the Craving Game, watch a movie, call a friend, take a shower, exercise.

physical.title: 💪 Physical Activity
physical.description: Walk, run, yoga, dancing—anything to move your body and change your chemistry.

journaling.title: 📔 Journaling
journaling.description: Use the Diary feature to write through cravings and emotions.

emotions.title: 🎭 Emotional Processing
emotions.description: Track your moods, name your feelings, feel them without acting on them.

connection.title: 🤝 Connection
connection.description: Call someone, text a friend, attend a support group meeting.

coping.action: Create your personal Crisis Toolkit: Write down 3-5 things you'll do when cravings hit. Post it somewhere visible.

remove.title: 6. Remove Temptations
remove.intro: Make it as hard as possible to use. You can't say no to what isn't in front of you.

clean.title: 🚭 Clean Your Space
clean.description: Get rid of all supplies, paraphernalia, reminders. Deep clean your room, car, bag.

delete.title: 🗑️ Delete Access
delete.description: Delete apps, block websites, unfollow accounts, delete dealer contacts.

money.title: 💳 Limit Money
money.description: Give control of finances to someone you trust. Remove access cards if needed.

avoid.title: 📍 Avoid Locations
avoid.description: Plan routes that avoid your typical using spots. Go different ways.

distance.title: 👥 Distance from People
distance.description: Consider telling friends you're quitting—real friends will support you.

fortyEightHours.title: 7. Plan for the First 48 Hours
fortyEightHours.intro: The first 48 hours are the hardest. Plan it hour by hour if needed.

wakeUp.title: When You Wake Up
wakeUp.description: Tell someone your quit is starting. Do something healthy (exercise, meditation, healthy breakfast).

morning.title: Morning (After Sleep)
morning.description: Unstructured time is dangerous. Plan something: exercise, meet a friend, hobby, meditation.

afternoon.title: Afternoon (Peak Craving Time)
afternoon.description: Call your support person. Use your Crisis Toolkit. This time will pass.

evening.title: Evening (Lonely Time)
evening.description: Be around people or on the phone with someone. Stay occupied. Use the Diary to process.

night.title: At Night (Hardest for Many)
night.description: Prepare for sleep: warm bath, tea, meditation, audiobook. You can do this.

fortyEightHours.insight: 💡 If the first 48 hours pass without using, your odds of long-term success jump dramatically. You've got this.

mental.title: 8. Prepare Mentally
mental.intro: Your mindset determines your success. Plant these truths now, before you start:
beliefs: ✓ This is possible. Millions of people have done it. You can too.
✓ You're not weak. Addiction is powerful, and you're strong enough to overcome it.
✓ Cravings are temporary. They peak in 15-20 minutes then fade. You just have to ride the wave.
✓ Withdrawal is natural. Your body is healing. It will pass.
✓ Slips happen—but they don't erase your progress. One mistake doesn't mean failure. Get back up.
✓ You're worth this. Your health, freedom, and future are worth any temporary discomfort.
mental.action: Action: Write these truths down. Put them on your mirror. Read them daily.

doctor.title: 9. Talk to Your Doctor
doctor.intro: Especially important for alcohol and hard drugs. Medical supervision can make withdrawal safer and more manageable.

canProvide.title: What Your Doctor Can Provide:
items: Assessment of your addiction severity
Withdrawal management medication
Referral to addiction specialists
Treatment for underlying mental health issues
Medical detoxification if needed

beHonest.title: Be Honest With Your Doctor:
items: Tell them what, how much, and how often you've been using
Tell them your quit date
Tell them about any medical conditions
Tell them about medications you're taking

doctor.closing: This is not judgment—it's healthcare. Doctors help people quit every day. They want to help you.

tell.title: 10. Tell People (Carefully)
tell.intro: Accountability is powerful. But choose who you tell carefully.

positive.title: Tell These People:
items: Someone you completely trust
People who truly support you
Your support network
Your therapist or counselor

beCareful.title: Be Careful With:
items: People who use and might pressure you
People who've been judgmental about this before
People who aren't emotionally healthy themselves
Social media—you don't need to broadcast to everyone

tell.whatToSay: What to say: "I've decided to quit [addiction] on [date]. I'm telling you because I need your support. If I reach out struggling, please be there for me."

backup.title: 11. Create a Backup Plan
backup.intro: What if everything goes wrong? Have a plan for crisis moments.

cravingHits.title: If a Craving Hits Hard
steps: 2. Use your Crisis Toolkit (distraction, exercise, etc.)
3. Get to a safe place if needed
4. Use the Craving Game or Meditation feature

almostUse.title: If You Almost Use
steps: 2. Get professional help (therapist emergency line, doctor)
4. This is not failure—this is you fighting

doUse.title: If You Do Use
steps: 1. Don't beat yourself up or give up entirely
2. This is a lapse, not a relapse
5. Restart your quit date—you haven't failed

emergencyResources.title: Emergency Resources
emergencyResources.crisisText: Crisis Text Line: Text HOME to 741741

checklist.title: 12. Pre-Quit Checklist
checklist.intro: Before you start, make sure you've done these things:

items.quitDate: ✓ I've set a specific quit date (within 1-2 weeks)
items.triggerPlans: ✓ I've made plans for each trigger
items.supportPeople: ✓ I have 3-5 support people identified with their numbers saved
items.crisisToolkit: ✓ I've created my personal Crisis Toolkit (5+ strategies)
items.cleanSpace: ✓ I've removed temptations from my space
items.fortyEightHours: ✓ I've planned my first 48 hours hour by hour
items.doctor: ✓ I've talked to my doctor (or scheduled an appointment)
items.toldSupport: ✓ I've told at least one support person my quit date
items.helplines: ✓ I've saved crisis helpline numbers in my phone
items.ready: ✓ I understand that this will be hard—and I'm ready

placeholders.triggerPlans: Describe your plan for each trigger...
placeholders.supportPeople: List your support people and contact info...
placeholders.cleanSpace: Describe steps you've taken to clean your space...
placeholders.toldSupport: Who have you told about your quit date?...
placeholders.helplines: List the helpline numbers you've saved...
placeholders.ready: Your thoughts and feelings about being ready...

final.title: You're Ready
final.message1: You've made your plan. You've prepared your mind and your environment. You have support. You have tools. You know what's coming and you have strategies to handle it.
final.message2: The day you quit isn't the beginning of your recovery—it's just the beginning you can see. Recovery started when you decided to make this change. Every step you've taken to prepare is recovery.
final.message3: You're going to face difficult moments. Cravings will come. Emotions will be overwhelming. Withdrawal might be uncomfortable. But you're not doing this alone. You have people. You have tools. You have this app. You have yourself—and that's enough.
final.message4: Welcome to your new life. It starts now.
final.encouragement: When you're ready to quit, start tracking in this app. Use every tool available. Reach out to your support network. And remember: every single day you don't use is a victory worth celebrating.
